    base ¶

    Base interfaces.

    Every estimator in River is a class, and as such inherits from at least one base interface. These are used to categorize, organize, and standardize the many estimators that River contains.

    This module contains mixin classes, which are all suffixed by Mixin. Their purpose is to provide additional functionality to an estimator, and thus need to be used in conjunction with a non-mixin base class.

    This module also contains utilities for type hinting and tagging estimators.

    Modules:

    • base –
    • classifier –
    • clusterer –
    • drift_detector –

      Base classes for drift detection.

    • ensemble –
    • estimator –
    • multi_output –
    • regressor –

    • Ensemble –

      An ensemble is a model which is composed of a list of models.

      An estimator.

    • MiniBatchClassifier –

      A classifier that can operate on mini-batches.

    • MiniBatchMultiTargetRegressor –

      A multi-target regressor that can operate on mini-batches.

      A regressor that can operate on mini-batches.

    • MiniBatchSupervisedTransformer –

      A supervised transformer that can operate on mini-batches.

    • MiniBatchTransformer –

      A transform that can operate on mini-batches.

    • MultiLabelClassifier –

      Multi-label classifier.

    • MultiTargetRegressor –

      Multi-target regressor.

    • Regressor –

      A regressor.

    • SupervisedTransformer –

      A supervised transformer.

    • Transformer –

      A transformer.

    • Wrapper –

      A wrapper model.

    • WrapperEnsemble –

      A wrapper ensemble is an ensemble composed of multiple copies of the same model.

    Base ¶

    Base class that is inherited by the majority of classes in River.

    This base class allows us to handle the following tasks in a uniform manner:

    • Getting and setting parameters
    • Displaying information
    • Mutating/cloning

    Methods:

    • clone –

      Return a fresh estimator with the same parameters.

    • mutate –

      Modify attributes.

    clone ¶

    clone(
    new_params: dict[str, Any] | None = None,
    include_attributes: bool = False,
) -> Self

    Return a fresh estimator with the same parameters.

    The clone has the same parameters but has not been updated with any data.

    This works by looking at the parameters from the class signature. Each parameter is either

    • recursively cloned if its a class.
    • deep-copied via copy.deepcopy if not.

    If the calling object is stochastic (i.e. it accepts a seed parameter) and has not been seeded, then the clone will not be idempotent. Indeed, this method's purpose if simply to return a new instance with the same input parameters.

    Parameters¶

    new_params include_attributes Whether attributes that are not present in the class' signature should also be cloned or not.

    Examples¶

    from river import linear_model from river import optim

    model = linear_model.LinearRegression( ... optimizer=optim.SGD(lr=0.042), ... )

    new_params = { ... 'optimizer': optim.SGD(.001) ... }

    model.clone(new_params) LinearRegression ( optimizer=SGD ( lr=Constant ( learning_rate=0.001 ) ) loss=Squared () l2=0. l1=0. intercept_init=0. intercept_lr=Constant ( learning_rate=0.01 ) clip_gradient=1e+12 initializer=Zeros () )

    The algorithm is recursively called down Pipelines and TransformerUnions.

    from river import compose from river import preprocessing

    model = compose.Pipeline( ... preprocessing.StandardScaler(), ... linear_model.LinearRegression( ... optimizer=optim.SGD(0.042), ... ) ... )

    new_params = { ... 'LinearRegression': { ... 'optimizer': optim.SGD(0.03) ... } ... }

    model.clone(new_params) Pipeline ( StandardScaler ( with_std=True ), LinearRegression ( optimizer=SGD ( lr=Constant ( learning_rate=0.03 ) ) loss=Squared () l2=0. l1=0. intercept_init=0. intercept_lr=Constant ( learning_rate=0.01 ) clip_gradient=1e+12 initializer=Zeros () ) )

    mutate ¶

    mutate(new_attrs: dict[str, Any]) -> None

    Modify attributes.

    This changes parameters inplace. Although you can change attributes yourself, this is the recommended way to proceed. By default, all attributes are immutable, meaning they shouldn't be mutated. Calling mutate on an immutable attribute raises a ValueError. Mutable attributes are specified via the _mutable_attributes property, and are thus specified on a per-estimator basis.

    Parameters¶

    new_attrs

    Examples¶

    from river import linear_model from river import optim

    model = linear_model.LinearRegression( ... optimizer=optim.SGD(0.042), ... )

    new_params = { ... 'optimizer': {'lr': optim.schedulers.Constant(0.001)} ... }

    model.mutate(new_params) model LinearRegression ( optimizer=SGD ( lr=Constant ( learning_rate=0.001 ) ) loss=Squared () l2=0. l1=0. intercept_init=0. intercept_lr=Constant ( learning_rate=0.01 ) clip_gradient=1e+12 initializer=Zeros () )

    The algorithm is recursively called down Pipelines and TransformerUnions.

    from river import compose from river import preprocessing

    model = compose.Pipeline( ... preprocessing.StandardScaler(), ... linear_model.LinearRegression( ... optimizer=optim.SGD(lr=0.042), ... ) ... )

    new_params = { ... 'LinearRegression': { ... 'l2': 5, ... 'optimizer': {'lr': optim.schedulers.Constant(0.03)} ... } ... }

    model.mutate(new_params) model Pipeline ( StandardScaler ( with_std=True ), LinearRegression ( optimizer=SGD ( lr=Constant ( learning_rate=0.03 ) ) loss=Squared () l2=5 l1=0. intercept_init=0. intercept_lr=Constant ( learning_rate=0.01 ) clip_gradient=1e+12 initializer=Zeros () ) )

      Transform a set of features x.

) -> dict[FeatureName, Any]

    Parameters¶

    x A dictionary of features.

    Returns¶

    The transformed values.

    A binary drift detector that is also capable of issuing warnings.

    Methods:

    • clone –

      Return a fresh estimator with the same parameters.

    • mutate –

      Modify attributes.

    • update –

      Update the detector with a single boolean input.

    Attributes:

    • drift_detected (bool) –

      Whether or not a drift is detected following the last update.

    • warning_detected (bool) –

      Whether or not a drift is detected following the last update.

    clone ¶

    clone(
    new_params: dict[str, Any] | None = None,
    include_attributes: bool = False,
) -> Self

    Return a fresh estimator with the same parameters.

    The clone has the same parameters but has not been updated with any data.

    This works by looking at the parameters from the class signature. Each parameter is either

    • recursively cloned if its a class.
    • deep-copied via copy.deepcopy if not.

    If the calling object is stochastic (i.e. it accepts a seed parameter) and has not been seeded, then the clone will not be idempotent. Indeed, this method's purpose if simply to return a new instance with the same input parameters.

    Parameters¶

    new_params include_attributes Whether attributes that are not present in the class' signature should also be cloned or not.

    Examples¶

    from river import linear_model from river import optim

    model = linear_model.LinearRegression( ... optimizer=optim.SGD(lr=0.042), ... )

    new_params = { ... 'optimizer': optim.SGD(.001) ... }

    model.clone(new_params) LinearRegression ( optimizer=SGD ( lr=Constant ( learning_rate=0.001 ) ) loss=Squared () l2=0. l1=0. intercept_init=0. intercept_lr=Constant ( learning_rate=0.01 ) clip_gradient=1e+12 initializer=Zeros () )

    The algorithm is recursively called down Pipelines and TransformerUnions.

    from river import compose from river import preprocessing

    model = compose.Pipeline( ... preprocessing.StandardScaler(), ... linear_model.LinearRegression( ... optimizer=optim.SGD(0.042), ... ) ... )

    new_params = { ... 'LinearRegression': { ... 'optimizer': optim.SGD(0.03) ... } ... }

    model.clone(new_params) Pipeline ( StandardScaler ( with_std=True ), LinearRegression ( optimizer=SGD ( lr=Constant ( learning_rate=0.03 ) ) loss=Squared () l2=0. l1=0. intercept_init=0. intercept_lr=Constant ( learning_rate=0.01 ) clip_gradient=1e+12 initializer=Zeros () ) )

    mutate ¶

    mutate(new_attrs: dict[str, Any]) -> None

    Modify attributes.

    This changes parameters inplace. Although you can change attributes yourself, this is the recommended way to proceed. By default, all attributes are immutable, meaning they shouldn't be mutated. Calling mutate on an immutable attribute raises a ValueError. Mutable attributes are specified via the _mutable_attributes property, and are thus specified on a per-estimator basis.

    Parameters¶

    new_attrs

    Examples¶

    from river import linear_model from river import optim

    model = linear_model.LinearRegression( ... optimizer=optim.SGD(0.042), ... )

    new_params = { ... 'optimizer': {'lr': optim.schedulers.Constant(0.001)} ... }

    model.mutate(new_params) model LinearRegression ( optimizer=SGD ( lr=Constant ( learning_rate=0.001 ) ) loss=Squared () l2=0. l1=0. intercept_init=0. intercept_lr=Constant ( learning_rate=0.01 ) clip_gradient=1e+12 initializer=Zeros () )

    The algorithm is recursively called down Pipelines and TransformerUnions.

    from river import compose from river import preprocessing

    model = compose.Pipeline( ... preprocessing.StandardScaler(), ... linear_model.LinearRegression( ... optimizer=optim.SGD(lr=0.042), ... ) ... )

    new_params = { ... 'LinearRegression': { ... 'l2': 5, ... 'optimizer': {'lr': optim.schedulers.Constant(0.03)} ... } ... }

    clone ¶

    clone(
    new_params: dict[str, Any] | None = None,
    include_attributes: bool = False,
) -> Self

    Return a fresh estimator with the same parameters.

    The clone has the same parameters but has not been updated with any data.

    This works by looking at the parameters from the class signature. Each parameter is either

    • recursively cloned if its a class.
    • deep-copied via copy.deepcopy if not.

    If the calling object is stochastic (i.e. it accepts a seed parameter) and has not been seeded, then the clone will not be idempotent. Indeed, this method's purpose if simply to return a new instance with the same input parameters.

    Parameters¶

    new_params include_attributes Whether attributes that are not present in the class' signature should also be cloned or not.

    Examples¶

    from river import linear_model from river import optim

    model = linear_model.LinearRegression( ... optimizer=optim.SGD(lr=0.042), ... )

    new_params = { ... 'optimizer': optim.SGD(.001) ... }

    model.clone(new_params) LinearRegression ( optimizer=SGD ( lr=Constant ( learning_rate=0.001 ) ) loss=Squared () l2=0. l1=0. intercept_init=0. intercept_lr=Constant ( learning_rate=0.01 ) clip_gradient=1e+12 initializer=Zeros () )

    The algorithm is recursively called down Pipelines and TransformerUnions.

    from river import compose from river import preprocessing

    model = compose.Pipeline( ... preprocessing.StandardScaler(), ... linear_model.LinearRegression( ... optimizer=optim.SGD(0.042), ... ) ... )

    new_params = { ... 'LinearRegression': { ... 'optimizer': optim.SGD(0.03) ... } ... }

    model.clone(new_params) Pipeline ( StandardScaler ( with_std=True ), LinearRegression ( optimizer=SGD ( lr=Constant ( learning_rate=0.03 ) ) loss=Squared () l2=0. l1=0. intercept_init=0. intercept_lr=Constant ( learning_rate=0.01 ) clip_gradient=1e+12 initializer=Zeros () ) )

    mutate ¶

    mutate(new_attrs: dict[str, Any]) -> None

    Modify attributes.

    This changes parameters inplace. Although you can change attributes yourself, this is the recommended way to proceed. By default, all attributes are immutable, meaning they shouldn't be mutated. Calling mutate on an immutable attribute raises a ValueError. Mutable attributes are specified via the _mutable_attributes property, and are thus specified on a per-estimator basis.

    Parameters¶

    new_attrs

    Examples¶

    from river import linear_model from river import optim

    model = linear_model.LinearRegression( ... optimizer=optim.SGD(0.042), ... )

    new_params = { ... 'optimizer': {'lr': optim.schedulers.Constant(0.001)} ... }

    model.mutate(new_params) model LinearRegression ( optimizer=SGD ( lr=Constant ( learning_rate=0.001 ) ) loss=Squared () l2=0. l1=0. intercept_init=0. intercept_lr=Constant ( learning_rate=0.01 ) clip_gradient=1e+12 initializer=Zeros () )

    The algorithm is recursively called down Pipelines and TransformerUnions.

    from river import compose from river import preprocessing

    model = compose.Pipeline( ... preprocessing.StandardScaler(), ... linear_model.LinearRegression( ... optimizer=optim.SGD(lr=0.042), ... ) ... )

    new_params = { ... 'LinearRegression': { ... 'l2': 5, ... 'optimizer': {'lr': optim.schedulers.Constant(0.03)} ... } ... }

    model.mutate(new_params) model Pipeline ( StandardScaler ( with_std=True ), LinearRegression ( optimizer=SGD ( lr=Constant ( learning_rate=0.03 ) ) loss=Squared () l2=5 l1=0. intercept_init=0. intercept_lr=Constant ( learning_rate=0.01 ) clip_gradient=1e+12 initializer=Zeros () ) )

    clone ¶

    clone(
    new_params: dict[str, Any] | None = None,
    include_attributes: bool = False,
) -> Self

    Return a fresh estimator with the same parameters.

    The clone has the same parameters but has not been updated with any data.

    This works by looking at the parameters from the class signature. Each parameter is either

    • recursively cloned if its a class.
    • deep-copied via copy.deepcopy if not.

    If the calling object is stochastic (i.e. it accepts a seed parameter) and has not been seeded, then the clone will not be idempotent. Indeed, this method's purpose if simply to return a new instance with the same input parameters.

    Parameters¶

    new_params include_attributes Whether attributes that are not present in the class' signature should also be cloned or not.

    Examples¶

    from river import linear_model from river import optim

    model = linear_model.LinearRegression( ... optimizer=optim.SGD(lr=0.042), ... )

    new_params = { ... 'optimizer': optim.SGD(.001) ... }

    model.clone(new_params) LinearRegression ( optimizer=SGD ( lr=Constant ( learning_rate=0.001 ) ) loss=Squared () l2=0. l1=0. intercept_init=0. intercept_lr=Constant ( learning_rate=0.01 ) clip_gradient=1e+12 initializer=Zeros () )

    The algorithm is recursively called down Pipelines and TransformerUnions.

    from river import compose from river import preprocessing

    model = compose.Pipeline( ... preprocessing.StandardScaler(), ... linear_model.LinearRegression( ... optimizer=optim.SGD(0.042), ... ) ... )

    new_params = { ... 'LinearRegression': { ... 'optimizer': optim.SGD(0.03) ... } ... }

    model.clone(new_params) Pipeline ( StandardScaler ( with_std=True ), LinearRegression ( optimizer=SGD ( lr=Constant ( learning_rate=0.03 ) ) loss=Squared () l2=0. l1=0. intercept_init=0. intercept_lr=Constant ( learning_rate=0.01 ) clip_gradient=1e+12 initializer=Zeros () ) )

    learn_one abstractmethod ¶

    learn_one(
    x: dict[FeatureName, Any], y: ClfTarget
) -> None

    Update the model with a set of features x and a label y.

    Parameters¶

    x A dictionary of features. y A label.

    mutate ¶

    mutate(new_attrs: dict[str, Any]) -> None

    Modify attributes.

    This changes parameters inplace. Although you can change attributes yourself, this is the recommended way to proceed. By default, all attributes are immutable, meaning they shouldn't be mutated. Calling mutate on an immutable attribute raises a ValueError. Mutable attributes are specified via the _mutable_attributes property, and are thus specified on a per-estimator basis.

    Parameters¶

    new_attrs

    Examples¶

    from river import linear_model from river import optim

    model = linear_model.LinearRegression( ... optimizer=optim.SGD(0.042), ... )

    new_params = { ... 'optimizer': {'lr': optim.schedulers.Constant(0.001)} ... }

    model.mutate(new_params) model LinearRegression ( optimizer=SGD ( lr=Constant ( learning_rate=0.001 ) ) loss=Squared () l2=0. l1=0. intercept_init=0. intercept_lr=Constant ( learning_rate=0.01 ) clip_gradient=1e+12 initializer=Zeros () )

    The algorithm is recursively called down Pipelines and TransformerUnions.

    from river import compose from river import preprocessing

    model = compose.Pipeline( ... preprocessing.StandardScaler(), ... linear_model.LinearRegression( ... optimizer=optim.SGD(lr=0.042), ... ) ... )

    new_params = { ... 'LinearRegression': { ... 'l2': 5, ... 'optimizer': {'lr': optim.schedulers.Constant(0.03)} ... } ... }

    model.mutate(new_params) model Pipeline ( StandardScaler ( with_std=True ), LinearRegression ( optimizer=SGD ( lr=Constant ( learning_rate=0.03 ) ) loss=Squared () l2=5 l1=0. intercept_init=0. intercept_lr=Constant ( learning_rate=0.01 ) clip_gradient=1e+12 initializer=Zeros () ) )

    predict_one ¶

    predict_one(
    x: dict[FeatureName, Any], **kwargs: Any
) -> ClfTarget | None

    Predict the label of a set of features x.

    Parameters¶

    x A dictionary of features.

    Returns¶

    The predicted label.

    predict_proba_one ¶

    predict_proba_one(
    x: dict[FeatureName, Any], **kwargs: Any
) -> dict[ClfTarget, float]

    Predict the probability of each label for a dictionary of features x.

    Parameters¶

    x A dictionary of features.

    Returns¶

    A dictionary that associates a probability which each label.

    Clusterer ¶

    Bases: Estimator

    
              flowchart TD
              vendor.river.river.base.Clusterer[Clusterer]

              click vendor.river.river.base.Clusterer href "" "vendor.river.river.base.Clusterer"
              click vendor.river.river.base.estimator.Estimator href "" "vendor.river.river.base.estimator.Estimator"
              click vendor.river.river.base.base.Base href "" "vendor.river.river.base.base.Base"

    A clustering model.

    Methods:

    • clone –

      Return a fresh estimator with the same parameters.

    • learn_one –

      Update the model with a set of features x.

    • mutate –

      Modify attributes.

    • predict_one –

      Predicts the cluster number for a set of features x.

    clone ¶

    clone(
    new_params: dict[str, Any] | None = None,
    include_attributes: bool = False,
) -> Self

    Return a fresh estimator with the same parameters.

    The clone has the same parameters but has not been updated with any data.

    This works by looking at the parameters from the class signature. Each parameter is either

    • recursively cloned if its a class.
    • deep-copied via copy.deepcopy if not.

    If the calling object is stochastic (i.e. it accepts a seed parameter) and has not been seeded, then the clone will not be idempotent. Indeed, this method's purpose if simply to return a new instance with the same input parameters.

    Parameters¶

    new_params include_attributes Whether attributes that are not present in the class' signature should also be cloned or not.

    Examples¶

    from river import linear_model from river import optim

    model = linear_model.LinearRegression( ... optimizer=optim.SGD(lr=0.042), ... )

    new_params = { ... 'optimizer': optim.SGD(.001) ... }

    model.clone(new_params) LinearRegression ( optimizer=SGD ( lr=Constant ( learning_rate=0.001 ) ) loss=Squared () l2=0. l1=0. intercept_init=0. intercept_lr=Constant ( learning_rate=0.01 ) clip_gradient=1e+12 initializer=Zeros () )

    The algorithm is recursively called down Pipelines and TransformerUnions.

    from river import compose from river import preprocessing

    model = compose.Pipeline( ... preprocessing.StandardScaler(), ... linear_model.LinearRegression( ... optimizer=optim.SGD(0.042), ... ) ... )

    new_params = { ... 'LinearRegression': { ... 'optimizer': optim.SGD(0.03) ... } ... }

    model.clone(new_params) Pipeline ( StandardScaler ( with_std=True ), LinearRegression ( optimizer=SGD ( lr=Constant ( learning_rate=0.03 ) ) loss=Squared () l2=0. l1=0. intercept_init=0. intercept_lr=Constant ( learning_rate=0.01 ) clip_gradient=1e+12 initializer=Zeros () ) )

    learn_one abstractmethod ¶

    learn_one(x: dict[FeatureName, Any]) -> None

    Update the model with a set of features x.

    Parameters¶

    x A dictionary of features.

    mutate ¶

    mutate(new_attrs: dict[str, Any]) -> None

    Modify attributes.

    This changes parameters inplace. Although you can change attributes yourself, this is the recommended way to proceed. By default, all attributes are immutable, meaning they shouldn't be mutated. Calling mutate on an immutable attribute raises a ValueError. Mutable attributes are specified via the _mutable_attributes property, and are thus specified on a per-estimator basis.

    Parameters¶

    new_attrs

    Examples¶

    from river import linear_model from river import optim

    model = linear_model.LinearRegression( ... optimizer=optim.SGD(0.042), ... )

    new_params = { ... 'optimizer': {'lr': optim.schedulers.Constant(0.001)} ... }

    model.mutate(new_params) model LinearRegression ( optimizer=SGD ( lr=Constant ( learning_rate=0.001 ) ) loss=Squared () l2=0. l1=0. intercept_init=0. intercept_lr=Constant ( learning_rate=0.01 ) clip_gradient=1e+12 initializer=Zeros () )

    The algorithm is recursively called down Pipelines and TransformerUnions.

    from river import compose from river import preprocessing

    model = compose.Pipeline( ... preprocessing.StandardScaler(), ... linear_model.LinearRegression( ... optimizer=optim.SGD(lr=0.042), ... ) ... )

    new_params = { ... 'LinearRegression': { ... 'l2': 5, ... 'optimizer': {'lr': optim.schedulers.Constant(0.03)} ... } ... }

    model.mutate(new_params) model Pipeline ( StandardScaler ( with_std=True ), LinearRegression ( optimizer=SGD ( lr=Constant ( learning_rate=0.03 ) ) loss=Squared () l2=5 l1=0. intercept_init=0. intercept_lr=Constant ( learning_rate=0.01 ) clip_gradient=1e+12 initializer=Zeros () ) )

    predict_one abstractmethod ¶

    predict_one(x: dict[FeatureName, Any]) -> int

    Predicts the cluster number for a set of features x.

    Parameters¶

    x A dictionary of features.

    Returns¶

    A cluster number.

              click vendor.river.river.base.base.Base href "" "vendor.river.river.base.base.Base"

    A drift detector that is also capable of issuing warnings.

    Methods:

    • clone –

      Return a fresh estimator with the same parameters.

    • mutate –

      Modify attributes.

    • update –

      Update the detector with a single data point.

    Attributes:

    • drift_detected (bool) –

      Whether or not a drift is detected following the last update.

    • warning_detected (bool) –

      Whether or not a drift is detected following the last update.

    drift_detected property ¶

    drift_detected: bool

    Whether or not a drift is detected following the last update.

    warning_detected property ¶

    warning_detected: bool

    Whether or not a drift is detected following the last update.

    clone ¶

    clone(
    new_params: dict[str, Any] | None = None,
    include_attributes: bool = False,
) -> Self

    Return a fresh estimator with the same parameters.

    The clone has the same parameters but has not been updated with any data.

    This works by looking at the parameters from the class signature. Each parameter is either

    • recursively cloned if its a class.
    • deep-copied via copy.deepcopy if not.

    If the calling object is stochastic (i.e. it accepts a seed parameter) and has not been seeded, then the clone will not be idempotent. Indeed, this method's purpose if simply to return a new instance with the same input parameters.

    Parameters¶

    new_params include_attributes Whether attributes that are not present in the class' signature should also be cloned or not.

    Examples¶

    from river import linear_model from river import optim

    model = linear_model.LinearRegression( ... optimizer=optim.SGD(lr=0.042), ... )

    new_params = { ... 'optimizer': optim.SGD(.001) ... }

    model.clone(new_params) LinearRegression ( optimizer=SGD ( lr=Constant ( learning_rate=0.001 ) ) loss=Squared () l2=0. l1=0. intercept_init=0. intercept_lr=Constant ( learning_rate=0.01 ) clip_gradient=1e+12 initializer=Zeros () )

    The algorithm is recursively called down Pipelines and TransformerUnions.

    from river import compose from river import preprocessing

    model = compose.Pipeline( ... preprocessing.StandardScaler(), ... linear_model.LinearRegression( ... optimizer=optim.SGD(0.042), ... ) ... )

    new_params = { ... 'LinearRegression': { ... 'optimizer': optim.SGD(0.03) ... } ... }

    model.clone(new_params) Pipeline ( StandardScaler ( with_std=True ), LinearRegression ( optimizer=SGD ( lr=Constant ( learning_rate=0.03 ) ) loss=Squared () l2=0. l1=0. intercept_init=0. intercept_lr=Constant ( learning_rate=0.01 ) clip_gradient=1e+12 initializer=Zeros () ) )

    mutate ¶

    mutate(new_attrs: dict[str, Any]) -> None

    Modify attributes.

    This changes parameters inplace. Although you can change attributes yourself, this is the recommended way to proceed. By default, all attributes are immutable, meaning they shouldn't be mutated. Calling mutate on an immutable attribute raises a ValueError. Mutable attributes are specified via the _mutable_attributes property, and are thus specified on a per-estimator basis.

    Parameters¶

    new_attrs

    Examples¶

    from river import linear_model from river import optim

    model = linear_model.LinearRegression( ... optimizer=optim.SGD(0.042), ... )

    new_params = { ... 'optimizer': {'lr': optim.schedulers.Constant(0.001)} ... }

    model.mutate(new_params) model LinearRegression ( optimizer=SGD ( lr=Constant ( learning_rate=0.001 ) ) loss=Squared () l2=0. l1=0. intercept_init=0. intercept_lr=Constant ( learning_rate=0.01 ) clip_gradient=1e+12 initializer=Zeros () )

    The algorithm is recursively called down Pipelines and TransformerUnions.

    from river import compose from river import preprocessing

    new_params = { ... 'LinearRegression': { ... 'l2': 5, ... 'optimizer': {'lr': optim.schedulers.Constant(0.03)} ... } ... }

    model.mutate(new_params) model Pipeline ( StandardScaler ( with_std=True ), LinearRegression ( optimizer=SGD ( lr=Constant ( learning_rate=0.03 ) ) loss=Squared () l2=5 l1=0. intercept_init=0. intercept_lr=Constant ( learning_rate=0.01 ) clip_gradient=1e+12 initializer=Zeros () ) )

    clone ¶

    clone(
    new_params: dict[str, Any] | None = None,
    include_attributes: bool = False,
) -> Self

    Return a fresh estimator with the same parameters.

    The clone has the same parameters but has not been updated with any data.

    This works by looking at the parameters from the class signature. Each parameter is either

    • recursively cloned if its a class.
    • deep-copied via copy.deepcopy if not.

    If the calling object is stochastic (i.e. it accepts a seed parameter) and has not been seeded, then the clone will not be idempotent. Indeed, this method's purpose if simply to return a new instance with the same input parameters.

    Parameters¶

    new_params include_attributes Whether attributes that are not present in the class' signature should also be cloned or not.

    Examples¶

    from river import linear_model from river import optim

Calling mutate on an immutable attribute raises a ValueError. Mutable attributes are specified via the _mutable_attributes property, and are thus specified on a per-estimator basis.

    clone ¶

    clone(
    new_params: dict[str, Any] | None = None,
    include_attributes: bool = False,
) -> Self

    Return a fresh estimator with the same parameters.

    The clone has the same parameters but has not been updated with any data.

    This works by looking at the parameters from the class signature. Each parameter is either

    • recursively cloned if its a class.
    • deep-copied via copy.deepcopy if not.

    If the calling object is stochastic (i.e. it accepts a seed parameter) and has not been seeded, then the clone will not be idempotent. Indeed, this method's purpose if simply to return a new instance with the same input parameters.

    Parameters¶

    new_params include_attributes Whether attributes that are not present in the class' signature should also be cloned or not.

    Examples¶

    from river import linear_model from river import optim

    model = linear_model.LinearRegression( ... optimizer=optim.SGD(lr=0.042), ... )

    new_params = { ... 'optimizer': optim.SGD(.001) ... }

    model.clone(new_params) LinearRegression ( optimizer=SGD ( lr=Constant ( learning_rate=0.001 ) ) loss=Squared () l2=0. l1=0. intercept_init=0. intercept_lr=Constant ( learning_rate=0.01 ) clip_gradient=1e+12 initializer=Zeros () )

              click vendor.river.river.base.base.Base href "" "vendor.river.river.base.base.Base"

    A classifier that can operate on mini-batches.

    Methods:

    • clone –

      Return a fresh estimator with the same parameters.

    • learn_many –

      Update the model with a mini-batch of features X and boolean targets y.

    • learn_one –

      Update the model with a set of features x and a label y.

    • mutate –

      Modify attributes.

    • predict_many –

      Predict the outcome for each given sample.

    • predict_one –

      Predict the label of a set of features x.

    • predict_proba_many –

      Predict the outcome probabilities for each given sample.

    • predict_proba_one –

      Predict the probability of each label for a dictionary of features x.

    clone ¶

    clone(
    new_params: dict[str, Any] | None = None,
    include_attributes: bool = False,
) -> Self

    Return a fresh estimator with the same parameters.

    The clone has the same parameters but has not been updated with any data.

    This works by looking at the parameters from the class signature. Each parameter is either

    • recursively cloned if its a class.
    • deep-copied via copy.deepcopy if not.

    If the calling object is stochastic (i.e. it accepts a seed parameter) and has not been seeded, then the clone will not be idempotent. Indeed, this method's purpose if simply to return a new instance with the same input parameters.

    Parameters¶

    new_params include_attributes Whether attributes that are not present in the class' signature should also be cloned or not.

    Examples¶

    from river import linear_model from river import optim

    model = linear_model.LinearRegression( ... optimizer=optim.SGD(lr=0.042), ... )

    new_params = { ... 'optimizer': optim.SGD(.001) ... }

    model.clone(new_params) LinearRegression ( optimizer=SGD ( lr=Constant ( learning_rate=0.001 ) ) loss=Squared () l2=0. l1=0. intercept_init=0. intercept_lr=Constant ( learning_rate=0.01 ) clip_gradient=1e+12 initializer=Zeros () )

    The algorithm is recursively called down Pipelines and TransformerUnions.

    from river import compose from river import preprocessing

    model = compose.Pipeline( ... preprocessing.StandardScaler(), ... linear_model.LinearRegression( ... optimizer=optim.SGD(0.042), ... ) ... )

    new_params = { ... 'LinearRegression': { ... 'optimizer': optim.SGD(0.03) ... } ... }

    model.clone(new_params) Pipeline ( StandardScaler ( with_std=True ), LinearRegression ( optimizer=SGD ( lr=Constant ( learning_rate=0.03 ) ) loss=Squared () l2=0. l1=0. intercept_init=0. intercept_lr=Constant ( learning_rate=0.01 ) clip_gradient=1e+12 initializer=Zeros () ) )

    learn_many abstractmethod ¶

    learn_many(X: DataFrame, y: Series) -> None

    Update the model with a mini-batch of features X and boolean targets y.

    Parameters¶

    X A dataframe of features. y A series of boolean target values.

    learn_one abstractmethod ¶

    learn_one(
    x: dict[FeatureName, Any], y: ClfTarget
) -> None

    Update the model with a set of features x and a label y.

    Parameters¶

    x A dictionary of features. y A label.

    mutate ¶

    mutate(new_attrs: dict[str, Any]) -> None

    Modify attributes.

    This changes parameters inplace. Although you can change attributes yourself, this is the recommended way to proceed. By default, all attributes are immutable, meaning they shouldn't be mutated. Calling mutate on an immutable attribute raises a ValueError. Mutable attributes are specified via the _mutable_attributes property, and are thus specified on a per-estimator basis.

    Parameters¶

    new_attrs

    Examples¶

    from river import linear_model from river import optim

    model = linear_model.LinearRegression( ... optimizer=optim.SGD(0.042), ... )

    new_params = { ... 'optimizer': {'lr': optim.schedulers.Constant(0.001)} ... }

    model.mutate(new_params) model LinearRegression ( optimizer=SGD ( lr=Constant ( learning_rate=0.001 ) ) loss=Squared () l2=0. l1=0. intercept_init=0. intercept_lr=Constant ( learning_rate=0.01 ) clip_gradient=1e+12 initializer=Zeros () )

    The algorithm is recursively called down Pipelines and TransformerUnions.

    from river import compose from river import preprocessing

    model = compose.Pipeline( ... preprocessing.StandardScaler(), ... linear_model.LinearRegression( ... optimizer=optim.SGD(lr=0.042), ... ) ... )

    new_params = { ... 'LinearRegression': { ... 'l2': 5, ... 'optimizer': {'lr': optim.schedulers.Constant(0.03)} ... } ... }

    model.mutate(new_params) model Pipeline ( StandardScaler ( with_std=True ), LinearRegression ( optimizer=SGD ( lr=Constant ( learning_rate=0.03 ) ) loss=Squared () l2=5 l1=0. intercept_init=0. intercept_lr=Constant ( learning_rate=0.01 ) clip_gradient=1e+12 initializer=Zeros () ) )

    predict_many ¶

    predict_many(X: DataFrame) -> Series

    Predict the outcome for each given sample.

    Parameters¶

    X A dataframe of features.

    Returns¶

    The predicted labels.

    predict_one ¶

    predict_one(
    x: dict[FeatureName, Any], **kwargs: Any
) -> ClfTarget | None

    Predict the label of a set of features x.

    Parameters¶

    x A dictionary of features.

    Returns¶

    The predicted label.

    predict_proba_many ¶

    predict_proba_many(X: DataFrame) -> DataFrame

    Predict the outcome probabilities for each given sample.

    Parameters¶

    X A dataframe of features.

    Returns¶

    A dataframe with probabilities of True and False for each sample.

    predict_proba_one ¶

    predict_proba_one(
    x: dict[FeatureName, Any], **kwargs: Any
) -> dict[ClfTarget, float]

    Predict the probability of each label for a dictionary of features x.

    Parameters¶

    x A dictionary of features.

    Returns¶

    A dictionary that associates a probability which each label.

    MiniBatchMultiTargetRegressor ¶

    Bases: MultiTargetRegressor

    
              flowchart TD
              vendor.river.river.base.MiniBatchMultiTargetRegressor[MiniBatchMultiTargetRegressor]
              vendor.river.river.base.multi_output.MultiTargetRegressor[MultiTargetRegressor]
              vendor.river.river.base.estimator.Estimator[Estimator]
              vendor.river.river.base.base.Base[Base]

                              vendor.river.river.base.multi_output.MultiTargetRegressor --> vendor.river.river.base.MiniBatchMultiTargetRegressor
                                vendor.river.river.base.estimator.Estimator --> vendor.river.river.base.multi_output.MultiTargetRegressor
                                vendor.river.river.base.base.Base --> vendor.river.river.base.estimator.Estimator
                




              click vendor.river.river.base.MiniBatchMultiTargetRegressor href "" "vendor.river.river.base.MiniBatchMultiTargetRegressor"
              click vendor.river.river.base.multi_output.MultiTargetRegressor href "" "vendor.river.river.base.multi_output.MultiTargetRegressor"
              click vendor.river.river.base.estimator.Estimator href "" "vendor.river.river.base.estimator.Estimator"
              click vendor.river.river.base.base.Base href "" "vendor.river.river.base.base.Base"

    A multi-target regressor that can operate on mini-batches.

    Methods:

    • clone –

      Return a fresh estimator with the same parameters.

    • learn_many –

      Update the model with a mini-batch of features X and targets Y.

    • learn_one –

      Fits to a set of features x and a real-valued target y.

    • mutate –

      Modify attributes.

    • predict_one –

      Predict the outputs of features x.

    clone ¶

    clone(
    new_params: dict[str, Any] | None = None,
    include_attributes: bool = False,
) -> Self

    Return a fresh estimator with the same parameters.

    The clone has the same parameters but has not been updated with any data.

    This works by looking at the parameters from the class signature. Each parameter is either

    • recursively cloned if its a class.
    • deep-copied via copy.deepcopy if not.

    If the calling object is stochastic (i.e. it accepts a seed parameter) and has not been seeded, then the clone will not be idempotent. Indeed, this method's purpose if simply to return a new instance with the same input parameters.

    Parameters¶

    new_params include_attributes Whether attributes that are not present in the class' signature should also be cloned or not.

    Examples¶

    from river import linear_model from river import optim

    model = linear_model.LinearRegression( ... optimizer=optim.SGD(lr=0.042), ... )

    new_params = { ... 'optimizer': optim.SGD(.001) ... }

    model.clone(new_params) LinearRegression ( optimizer=SGD ( lr=Constant ( learning_rate=0.001 ) ) loss=Squared () l2=0. l1=0. intercept_init=0. intercept_lr=Constant ( learning_rate=0.01 ) clip_gradient=1e+12 initializer=Zeros () )

    The algorithm is recursively called down Pipelines and TransformerUnions.

    from river import compose from river import preprocessing

    model = compose.Pipeline( ... preprocessing.StandardScaler(), ... linear_model.LinearRegression( ... optimizer=optim.SGD(0.042), ... ) ... )

    new_params = { ... 'LinearRegression': { ... 'optimizer': optim.SGD(0.03) ... } ... }

    model.clone(new_params) Pipeline ( StandardScaler ( with_std=True ), LinearRegression ( optimizer=SGD ( lr=Constant ( learning_rate=0.03 ) ) loss=Squared () l2=0. l1=0. intercept_init=0. intercept_lr=Constant ( learning_rate=0.01 ) clip_gradient=1e+12 initializer=Zeros () ) )

    learn_many ¶

    learn_many(X: DataFrame, Y: DataFrame) -> None

    Update the model with a mini-batch of features X and targets Y.

    Parameters¶

    X A dataframe of features. Y A dataframe of targets.

    learn_one abstractmethod ¶

    learn_one(
    x: dict[FeatureName, Any],
    y: dict[FeatureName, RegTarget],
    **kwargs: Any
) -> None

    Fits to a set of features x and a real-valued target y.

    Parameters¶

    x A dictionary of features. y A dictionary of numeric targets.

    mutate ¶

    mutate(new_attrs: dict[str, Any]) -> None

    Modify attributes.

    This changes parameters inplace. Although you can change attributes yourself, this is the recommended way to proceed. By default, all attributes are immutable, meaning they shouldn't be mutated. Calling mutate on an immutable attribute raises a ValueError. Mutable attributes are specified via the _mutable_attributes property, and are thus specified on a per-estimator basis.

    Parameters¶

    new_attrs

    Examples¶

    from river import linear_model from river import optim

    model = linear_model.LinearRegression( ... optimizer=optim.SGD(0.042), ... )

    new_params = { ... 'optimizer': {'lr': optim.schedulers.Constant(0.001)} ... }

    model.mutate(new_params) model LinearRegression ( optimizer=SGD ( lr=Constant ( learning_rate=0.001 ) ) loss=Squared () l2=0. l1=0. intercept_init=0. intercept_lr=Constant ( learning_rate=0.01 ) clip_gradient=1e+12 initializer=Zeros () )

    The algorithm is recursively called down Pipelines and TransformerUnions.

    from river import compose from river import preprocessing

    model = compose.Pipeline( ... preprocessing.StandardScaler(), ... linear_model.LinearRegression( ... optimizer=optim.SGD(lr=0.042), ... ) ... )

    new_params = { ... 'LinearRegression': { ... 'l2': 5, ... 'optimizer': {'lr': optim.schedulers.Constant(0.03)} ... } ... }

    model.mutate(new_params) model Pipeline ( StandardScaler ( with_std=True ), LinearRegression ( optimizer=SGD ( lr=Constant ( learning_rate=0.03 ) ) loss=Squared () l2=5 l1=0. intercept_init=0. intercept_lr=Constant ( learning_rate=0.01 ) clip_gradient=1e+12 initializer=Zeros () ) )

    predict_one abstractmethod ¶

    predict_one(
    x: dict[FeatureName, Any],
) -> dict[FeatureName, RegTarget]

    Predict the outputs of features x.

    Parameters¶

    x A dictionary of features.

    Returns¶

    The predictions.

    A regressor that can operate on mini-batches.

    Methods:

    • clone –

      Return a fresh estimator with the same parameters.

    • learn_many –

      Update the model with a mini-batch of features X and real-valued targets y.

    • learn_one –

      Fits to a set of features x and a real-valued target y.

    • mutate –

      Modify attributes.

    • predict_many –

      Predict the outcome for each given sample.

    • predict_one –

      Predict the output of features x.

    clone ¶

    clone(
    new_params: dict[str, Any] | None = None,
    include_attributes: bool = False,
) -> Self

    Return a fresh estimator with the same parameters.

    The clone has the same parameters but has not been updated with any data.

    This works by looking at the parameters from the class signature. Each parameter is either

    • recursively cloned if its a class.
    • deep-copied via copy.deepcopy if not.

    If the calling object is stochastic (i.e. it accepts a seed parameter) and has not been seeded, then the clone will not be idempotent. Indeed, this method's purpose if simply to return a new instance with the same input parameters.

    Parameters¶

    new_params include_attributes Whether attributes that are not present in the class' signature should also be cloned or not.

    Examples¶

    from river import linear_model from river import optim

    model = linear_model.LinearRegression( ... optimizer=optim.SGD(lr=0.042), ... )

    new_params = { ... 'optimizer': optim.SGD(.001) ... }

    model.clone(new_params) LinearRegression ( optimizer=SGD ( lr=Constant ( learning_rate=0.001 ) ) loss=Squared () l2=0. l1=0. intercept_init=0. intercept_lr=Constant ( learning_rate=0.01 ) clip_gradient=1e+12 initializer=Zeros () )

    The algorithm is recursively called down Pipelines and TransformerUnions.

    from river import compose from river import preprocessing

    model = compose.Pipeline( ... preprocessing.StandardScaler(), ... linear_model.LinearRegression( ... optimizer=optim.SGD(0.042), ... ) ... )

    new_params = { ... 'LinearRegression': { ... 'optimizer': optim.SGD(0.03) ... } ... }

    model.clone(new_params) Pipeline ( StandardScaler ( with_std=True ), LinearRegression ( optimizer=SGD ( lr=Constant ( learning_rate=0.03 ) ) loss=Squared () l2=0. l1=0. intercept_init=0. intercept_lr=Constant ( learning_rate=0.01 ) clip_gradient=1e+12 initializer=Zeros () ) )

    learn_many abstractmethod ¶

    learn_many(X: DataFrame, y: Series) -> None

    Update the model with a mini-batch of features X and real-valued targets y.

    Parameters¶

    X A dataframe of features. y A series of numbers.

    learn_one abstractmethod ¶

    learn_one(
    x: dict[FeatureName, Any], y: RegTarget
) -> None

    Fits to a set of features x and a real-valued target y.

    Parameters¶

    mutate ¶

    mutate(new_attrs: dict[str, Any]) -> None

    Modify attributes.

    This changes parameters inplace. Although you can change attributes yourself, this is the recommended way to proceed. By default, all attributes are immutable, meaning they shouldn't be mutated. Calling mutate on an immutable attribute raises a ValueError. Mutable attributes are specified via the _mutable_attributes property, and are thus specified on a per-estimator basis.

    Parameters¶

    new_attrs

    Examples¶

    from river import linear_model from river import optim

    model = linear_model.LinearRegression( ... optimizer=optim.SGD(0.042), ... )

    new_params = { ... 'optimizer': {'lr': optim.schedulers.Constant(0.001)} ... }

    model.mutate(new_params) model LinearRegression ( optimizer=SGD ( lr=Constant ( learning_rate=0.001 ) ) loss=Squared () l2=0. l1=0. intercept_init=0. intercept_lr=Constant ( learning_rate=0.01 ) clip_gradient=1e+12 initializer=Zeros () )

    The algorithm is recursively called down Pipelines and TransformerUnions.

    from river import compose from river import preprocessing

    new_params = { ... 'LinearRegression': { ... 'l2': 5, ... 'optimizer': {'lr': optim.schedulers.Constant(0.03)} ... } ... }

    Bases: Transformer

              click vendor.river.river.base.MiniBatchSupervisedTransformer href "" "vendor.river.river.base.MiniBatchSupervisedTransformer"

    A supervised transformer that can operate on mini-batches.

    Methods:

    • learn_many –

      Update the model with a mini-batch of features X and targets y.

    • learn_one –

      Update with a set of features x.

    • transform_many –

      Transform a mini-batch of features.

    • transform_one –

      Transform a set of features x.

    learn_many abstractmethod ¶

    learn_many(X: DataFrame, y: Series) -> None

    Update the model with a mini-batch of features X and targets y.

    Parameters¶

    X A dataframe of features. y A series of boolean target values.

    learn_one ¶

    learn_one(x: dict[FeatureName, Any]) -> None

    Update with a set of features x.

    A lot of transformers don't actually have to do anything during the learn_one step because they are stateless. For this reason the default behavior of this function is to do nothing. Transformers that however do something during the learn_one can override this method.

    Parameters¶

    x A dictionary of features.

    transform_many abstractmethod ¶

    transform_many(X: DataFrame) -> DataFrame

    Transform a mini-batch of features.

    Parameters¶

    X A DataFrame of features.

    Returns¶

    A new DataFrame.

    transform_one abstractmethod ¶

    transform_one(
    x: dict[FeatureName, Any],
) -> dict[FeatureName, Any]

    Transform a set of features x.

    Parameters¶

    x A dictionary of features.

    Returns¶

    The transformed values.

    Bases: Transformer

    learn_many ¶

    learn_many(X: DataFrame) -> None

    Update with a mini-batch of features.

    A lot of transformers don't actually have to do anything during the learn_many step because they are stateless. For this reason the default behavior of this function is to do nothing. Transformers that however do something during the learn_many can override this method.

    Parameters¶

    X A DataFrame of features.

    learn_one ¶

    learn_one(x: dict[FeatureName, Any]) -> None

    Update with a set of features x.

    A lot of transformers don't actually have to do anything during the learn_one step because they are stateless. For this reason the default behavior of this function is to do nothing. Transformers that however do something during the learn_one can override this method.

    Parameters¶

    x A dictionary of features.

    Multi-label classifier.

    Methods:

    • clone –

      Return a fresh estimator with the same parameters.

    • learn_one –

      Update the model with a set of features x and the labels y.

    • mutate –

      Modify attributes.

    • predict_one –

      Predict the labels of a set of features x.

    • predict_proba_one –

      Predict the probability of each label appearing given dictionary of features x.

    clone ¶

    clone(
    new_params: dict[str, Any] | None = None,
    include_attributes: bool = False,
) -> Self

    Return a fresh estimator with the same parameters.

    The clone has the same parameters but has not been updated with any data.

    This works by looking at the parameters from the class signature. Each parameter is either

    • recursively cloned if its a class.
    • deep-copied via copy.deepcopy if not.

    If the calling object is stochastic (i.e. it accepts a seed parameter) and has not been seeded, then the clone will not be idempotent. Indeed, this method's purpose if simply to return a new instance with the same input parameters.

    Parameters¶

    new_params include_attributes Whether attributes that are not present in the class' signature should also be cloned or not.

    Examples¶

    from river import linear_model from river import optim

    model = linear_model.LinearRegression( ... optimizer=optim.SGD(lr=0.042), ... )

    new_params = { ... 'optimizer': optim.SGD(.001) ... }

    model.clone(new_params) LinearRegression ( optimizer=SGD ( lr=Constant ( learning_rate=0.001 ) ) loss=Squared () l2=0. l1=0. intercept_init=0. intercept_lr=Constant ( learning_rate=0.01 ) clip_gradient=1e+12 initializer=Zeros () )

    The algorithm is recursively called down Pipelines and TransformerUnions.

    from river import compose from river import preprocessing

    model = compose.Pipeline( ... preprocessing.StandardScaler(), ... linear_model.LinearRegression( ... optimizer=optim.SGD(0.042), ... ) ... )

    new_params = { ... 'LinearRegression': { ... 'optimizer': optim.SGD(0.03) ... } ... }

    model.clone(new_params) Pipeline ( StandardScaler ( with_std=True ), LinearRegression ( optimizer=SGD ( lr=Constant ( learning_rate=0.03 ) ) loss=Squared () l2=0. l1=0. intercept_init=0. intercept_lr=Constant ( learning_rate=0.01 ) clip_gradient=1e+12 initializer=Zeros () ) )

    learn_one abstractmethod ¶

    learn_one(
    x: dict[FeatureName, Any], y: dict[FeatureName, bool]
) -> None

    Update the model with a set of features x and the labels y.

    Parameters¶

    x A dictionary of features. y A dictionary of labels.

    mutate ¶

    mutate(new_attrs: dict[str, Any]) -> None

    Modify attributes.

    This changes parameters inplace. Although you can change attributes yourself, this is the recommended way to proceed. By default, all attributes are immutable, meaning they shouldn't be mutated. Calling mutate on an immutable attribute raises a ValueError. Mutable attributes are specified via the _mutable_attributes property, and are thus specified on a per-estimator basis.

    Parameters¶

    new_attrs

    Examples¶

    from river import linear_model from river import optim

    model = linear_model.LinearRegression( ... optimizer=optim.SGD(0.042), ... )

    new_params = { ... 'optimizer': {'lr': optim.schedulers.Constant(0.001)} ... }

    model.mutate(new_params) model LinearRegression ( optimizer=SGD ( lr=Constant ( learning_rate=0.001 ) ) loss=Squared () l2=0. l1=0. intercept_init=0. intercept_lr=Constant ( learning_rate=0.01 ) clip_gradient=1e+12 initializer=Zeros () )

    The algorithm is recursively called down Pipelines and TransformerUnions.

    from river import compose from river import preprocessing

    model = compose.Pipeline( ... preprocessing.StandardScaler(), ... linear_model.LinearRegression( ... optimizer=optim.SGD(lr=0.042), ... ) ... )

    new_params = { ... 'LinearRegression': { ... 'l2': 5, ... 'optimizer': {'lr': optim.schedulers.Constant(0.03)} ... } ... }

    model.mutate(new_params) model Pipeline ( StandardScaler ( with_std=True ), LinearRegression ( optimizer=SGD ( lr=Constant ( learning_rate=0.03 ) ) loss=Squared () l2=5 l1=0. intercept_init=0. intercept_lr=Constant ( learning_rate=0.01 ) clip_gradient=1e+12 initializer=Zeros () ) )

    predict_one ¶

    predict_one(
    x: dict[FeatureName, Any], **kwargs: Any
) -> dict[FeatureName, bool]

    Predict the labels of a set of features x.

    Parameters¶

    x A dictionary of features.

    Returns¶

    The predicted labels.

    predict_proba_one ¶

    predict_proba_one(
    x: dict[FeatureName, Any], **kwargs: Any
) -> dict[FeatureName, dict[bool, float]]

    Predict the probability of each label appearing given dictionary of features x.

    A dictionary that associates a probability which each label.

    Multi-target regressor.

    • clone –

      Return a fresh estimator with the same parameters.

      Fits to a set of features x and a real-valued target y.

    • mutate –

      Modify attributes.

    • predict_one –

      Predict the outputs of features x.

    clone ¶

    clone(
    new_params: dict[str, Any] | None = None,
    include_attributes: bool = False,
) -> Self

    Return a fresh estimator with the same parameters.

    The clone has the same parameters but has not been updated with any data.

    This works by looking at the parameters from the class signature. Each parameter is either

    • recursively cloned if its a class.
    • deep-copied via copy.deepcopy if not.

    If the calling object is stochastic (i.e. it accepts a seed parameter) and has not been seeded, then the clone will not be idempotent. Indeed, this method's purpose if simply to return a new instance with the same input parameters.

    Parameters¶

    new_params include_attributes Whether attributes that are not present in the class' signature should also be cloned or not.

    Examples¶

    from river import linear_model from river import optim

    model = linear_model.LinearRegression( ... optimizer=optim.SGD(lr=0.042), ... )

    new_params = { ... 'optimizer': optim.SGD(.001) ... }

    model.clone(new_params) LinearRegression ( optimizer=SGD ( lr=Constant ( learning_rate=0.001 ) ) loss=Squared () l2=0. l1=0. intercept_init=0. intercept_lr=Constant ( learning_rate=0.01 ) clip_gradient=1e+12 initializer=Zeros () )

    The algorithm is recursively called down Pipelines and TransformerUnions.

    from river import compose from river import preprocessing

    model = compose.Pipeline( ... preprocessing.StandardScaler(), ... linear_model.LinearRegression( ... optimizer=optim.SGD(0.042), ... ) ... )

    new_params = { ... 'LinearRegression': { ... 'optimizer': optim.SGD(0.03) ... } ... }

    model.clone(new_params) Pipeline ( StandardScaler ( with_std=True ), LinearRegression ( optimizer=SGD ( lr=Constant ( learning_rate=0.03 ) ) loss=Squared () l2=0. l1=0. intercept_init=0. intercept_lr=Constant ( learning_rate=0.01 ) clip_gradient=1e+12 initializer=Zeros () ) )

    learn_one abstractmethod ¶

    learn_one(
    x: dict[FeatureName, Any],
    y: dict[FeatureName, RegTarget],
    **kwargs: Any
) -> None

    Fits to a set of features x and a real-valued target y.

    Parameters¶

    x A dictionary of features. y A dictionary of numeric targets.

    mutate ¶

    mutate(new_attrs: dict[str, Any]) -> None

    Modify attributes.

    This changes parameters inplace. Although you can change attributes yourself, this is the recommended way to proceed. By default, all attributes are immutable, meaning they shouldn't be mutated. Calling mutate on an immutable attribute raises a ValueError. Mutable attributes are specified via the _mutable_attributes property, and are thus specified on a per-estimator basis.

    Parameters¶

    new_attrs

    Examples¶

    from river import linear_model from river import optim

    model = linear_model.LinearRegression( ... optimizer=optim.SGD(0.042), ... )

    new_params = { ... 'optimizer': {'lr': optim.schedulers.Constant(0.001)} ... }

    model.mutate(new_params) model LinearRegression ( optimizer=SGD ( lr=Constant ( learning_rate=0.001 ) ) loss=Squared () l2=0. l1=0. intercept_init=0. intercept_lr=Constant ( learning_rate=0.01 ) clip_gradient=1e+12 initializer=Zeros () )

    The algorithm is recursively called down Pipelines and TransformerUnions.

    from river import compose from river import preprocessing

    model = compose.Pipeline( ... preprocessing.StandardScaler(), ... linear_model.LinearRegression( ... optimizer=optim.SGD(lr=0.042), ... ) ... )

    new_params = { ... 'LinearRegression': { ... 'l2': 5, ... 'optimizer': {'lr': optim.schedulers.Constant(0.03)} ... } ... }

    model.mutate(new_params) model Pipeline ( StandardScaler ( with_std=True ), LinearRegression ( optimizer=SGD ( lr=Constant ( learning_rate=0.03 ) ) loss=Squared () l2=5 l1=0. intercept_init=0. intercept_lr=Constant ( learning_rate=0.01 ) clip_gradient=1e+12 initializer=Zeros () ) )

    predict_one abstractmethod ¶

    predict_one(
    x: dict[FeatureName, Any],
) -> dict[FeatureName, RegTarget]

    Predict the outputs of features x.

    Parameters¶

    x A dictionary of features.

    Returns¶

    The predictions.

    Regressor ¶

    Bases: Estimator

              click vendor.river.river.base.Regressor href "" "vendor.river.river.base.Regressor"
              click vendor.river.river.base.estimator.Estimator href "" "vendor.river.river.base.estimator.Estimator"
              click vendor.river.river.base.base.Base href "" "vendor.river.river.base.base.Base"

    A regressor.

    Methods:

    • clone –

      Return a fresh estimator with the same parameters.

    • learn_one –

      Fits to a set of features x and a real-valued target y.

    • mutate –

      Modify attributes.

    • predict_one –

      Predict the output of features x.

    clone ¶

    clone(
    new_params: dict[str, Any] | None = None,
    include_attributes: bool = False,
) -> Self

    Return a fresh estimator with the same parameters.

    The clone has the same parameters but has not been updated with any data.

    This works by looking at the parameters from the class signature. Each parameter is either

    • recursively cloned if its a class.
    • deep-copied via copy.deepcopy if not.

    If the calling object is stochastic (i.e. it accepts a seed parameter) and has not been seeded, then the clone will not be idempotent. Indeed, this method's purpose if simply to return a new instance with the same input parameters.

    Parameters¶

    new_params include_attributes Whether attributes that are not present in the class' signature should also be cloned or not.

    Examples¶

    from river import linear_model from river import optim

    model = linear_model.LinearRegression( ... optimizer=optim.SGD(lr=0.042), ... )

    new_params = { ... 'optimizer': optim.SGD(.001) ... }

    model.clone(new_params) LinearRegression ( optimizer=SGD ( lr=Constant ( learning_rate=0.001 ) ) loss=Squared () l2=0. l1=0. intercept_init=0. intercept_lr=Constant ( learning_rate=0.01 ) clip_gradient=1e+12 initializer=Zeros () )

    The algorithm is recursively called down Pipelines and TransformerUnions.

    from river import compose from river import preprocessing

    model = compose.Pipeline( ... preprocessing.StandardScaler(), ... linear_model.LinearRegression( ... optimizer=optim.SGD(0.042), ... ) ... )

    new_params = { ... 'LinearRegression': { ... 'optimizer': optim.SGD(0.03) ... } ... }

    model.clone(new_params) Pipeline ( StandardScaler ( with_std=True ), LinearRegression ( optimizer=SGD ( lr=Constant ( learning_rate=0.03 ) ) loss=Squared () l2=0. l1=0. intercept_init=0. intercept_lr=Constant ( learning_rate=0.01 ) clip_gradient=1e+12 initializer=Zeros () ) )

    learn_one abstractmethod ¶

    learn_one(
    x: dict[FeatureName, Any], y: RegTarget
) -> None

    Fits to a set of features x and a real-valued target y.

    Parameters¶

    x A dictionary of features. y A numeric target.

    mutate ¶

    mutate(new_attrs: dict[str, Any]) -> None

    Modify attributes.

    This changes parameters inplace. Although you can change attributes yourself, this is the recommended way to proceed. By default, all attributes are immutable, meaning they shouldn't be mutated. Calling mutate on an immutable attribute raises a ValueError. Mutable attributes are specified via the _mutable_attributes property, and are thus specified on a per-estimator basis.

    Parameters¶

    new_attrs

    Examples¶

    from river import linear_model from river import optim

    model = linear_model.LinearRegression( ... optimizer=optim.SGD(0.042), ... )

    new_params = { ... 'optimizer': {'lr': optim.schedulers.Constant(0.001)} ... }

    model.mutate(new_params) model LinearRegression ( optimizer=SGD ( lr=Constant ( learning_rate=0.001 ) ) loss=Squared () l2=0. l1=0. intercept_init=0. intercept_lr=Constant ( learning_rate=0.01 ) clip_gradient=1e+12 initializer=Zeros () )

    The algorithm is recursively called down Pipelines and TransformerUnions.

    from river import compose from river import preprocessing

    model = compose.Pipeline( ... preprocessing.StandardScaler(), ... linear_model.LinearRegression( ... optimizer=optim.SGD(lr=0.042), ... ) ... )

    new_params = { ... 'LinearRegression': { ... 'l2': 5, ... 'optimizer': {'lr': optim.schedulers.Constant(0.03)} ... } ... }

    model.mutate(new_params) model Pipeline ( StandardScaler ( with_std=True ), LinearRegression ( optimizer=SGD ( lr=Constant ( learning_rate=0.03 ) ) loss=Squared () l2=5 l1=0. intercept_init=0. intercept_lr=Constant ( learning_rate=0.01 ) clip_gradient=1e+12 initializer=Zeros () ) )

              click vendor.river.river.base.Transformer href "" "vendor.river.river.base.Transformer"
              click vendor.river.river.base.transformer.BaseTransformer href "" "vendor.river.river.base.transformer.BaseTransformer"

    A transformer.

    Methods:

    • learn_one –

      Update with a set of features x.

    • transform_one –

      Transform a set of features x.

    learn_one ¶

    learn_one(x: dict[FeatureName, Any]) -> None

    Update with a set of features x.

    A lot of transformers don't actually have to do anything during the learn_one step because they are stateless. For this reason the default behavior of this function is to do nothing. Transformers that however do something during the learn_one can override this method.

    Parameters¶

    x A dictionary of features.

    transform_one abstractmethod ¶

    transform_one(
    x: dict[FeatureName, Any],
) -> dict[FeatureName, Any]

    Transform a set of features x.

    Parameters¶

    x A dictionary of features.

    Returns¶

    The transformed values.

              click vendor.river.river.base.wrapper.Wrapper href "" "vendor.river.river.base.wrapper.Wrapper"

    A wrapper ensemble is an ensemble composed of multiple copies of the same model.

    Parameters¶

    model The model to copy. n_models The number of copies to make. seed Random number generator seed for reproducibility.
